A **Christian state** is a country that recognizes a form of [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") as its [official religion](/wiki/Official_religion "Official religion") and often has a **state church** (also called an **established church**),[[1]](#cite_note-Backhouse2011-1) which is a [Christian denomination](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination") that supports the government and is supported by the government.[[2]](#cite_note-Eberle2013-2)

Historically, the nations of [Armenia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Armenia_(antiquity) "Kingdom of Armenia (antiquity)"),[[3]](#cite_note-MilmanMurdock1887-3)[[4]](#cite_note-ChingJarzombek2010-4) [Aksum](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Aksum "Kingdom of Aksum"), [Makuria](/wiki/Makuria "Makuria"), and the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") have declared themselves as Christian states, as well as the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") and its continuation the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"), the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), the [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire"), the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), the [Portuguese Empire](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire"), and the [Frankish Empire](/wiki/Frankish_Empire "Frankish Empire"), the [Belgian colonial empire](/wiki/Belgian_colonial_empire "Belgian colonial empire"), the [French empire](/wiki/French_colonial_empire "French colonial empire").[[5]](#cite_note-Ashby2010-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Frucht2004-6)

Today, several nations officially identify themselves as Christian states or have state churches. These countries include [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina"), [Armenia](/wiki/Armenia "Armenia"), [Costa Rica](/wiki/Costa_Rica "Costa Rica"), [El Salvador](/wiki/El_Salvador "El Salvador"),[[7]](#cite_note-YakobsonRubinstein2009-7) [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") (incl. [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland") and the [Faroes](/wiki/Faroe_Islands "Faroe Islands")),[[8]](#cite_note-Shadid1995-8) [England](/wiki/England "England"),[[9]](#cite_note-Joppke2013-9) [Dominican Republic](/wiki/Dominican_Republic "Dominican Republic"),[[10]](#cite_note-DOS2025-10) [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)"),[[11]](#cite_note-11) [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece"),[[12]](#cite_note-Jiang2012-12) [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary"),[[13]](#cite_note-Hungary's_Constitution_of_2011-13) [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland"),[[14]](#cite_note-Melton2005-14) [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein "Liechtenstein"),[[15]](#cite_note-Fox2008-15) [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta"),[[16]](#cite_note-legal-malta-16) [Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco"),[[17]](#cite_note-MonacoReligion-17) [Samoa](/wiki/Samoa "Samoa"),[[18]](#cite_note-Wyeth2017-18) [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia"),[[19]](#cite_note-19) [Tonga](/wiki/Tonga "Tonga"),[[20]](#cite_note-Fodor1986-20) [Tuvalu](/wiki/Tuvalu "Tuvalu"),[[21]](#cite_note-Temperman2010-21) [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City"),[[22]](#cite_note-Erasmus2010-22) and [Zambia](/wiki/Zambia "Zambia").[[23]](#cite_note-Jenkins2011-23) The laws of various Christian countries, such as those of Denmark, Norway, and Sweden require their monarch to be a Christian (usually of a particular denomination, such as [Evangelical Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism")).[[24]](#cite_note-Cimino2025-24)[[25]](#cite_note-Fandel2007-25)[[26]](#cite_note-KrunkeThorarensen2018-26) A Christian state stands in contrast to a [secular state](/wiki/Secular_state "Secular state"),[[27]](#cite_note-Boer2012-27) an [atheist state](/wiki/Atheist_state "Atheist state"),[[28]](#cite_note-MarxMcLellan2000-28) or another religious state, such as a [Jewish state](/wiki/Jewish_state "Jewish state"),[[29]](#cite_note-Burns1996-29) or an [Islamic state](/wiki/Islamic_state "Islamic state").[[30]](#cite_note-Sjoberg2006-30)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Justinian555AD.png/500px-Justinian555AD.png)](/wiki/File:Justinian555AD.png)

The boundaries of the Eastern Roman Empire under [Justinian the Great](/wiki/Justinian_the_Great "Justinian the Great")

The [Armenian Apostolic Church](/wiki/Armenian_Apostolic_Church "Armenian Apostolic Church") traces its origins to the apostolic era, asserting [apostolic succession](/wiki/Apostolic_succession "Apostolic succession") from the apostles [Bartholomew](/wiki/Bartholomew_the_Apostle "Bartholomew the Apostle")[[31]](#cite_note-31) and [Thaddeus (Jude)](/wiki/Jude_the_Apostle "Jude the Apostle").[[32]](#cite_note-Gilman-32)[[33]](#cite_note-Jacob-33)[[34]](#cite_note-Issaverdenz-34) The formal establishment of Christianity as the state religion of Armenia is traditionally dated to 301 AD, during the reign of [Tiridates III](/wiki/Tiridates_III_of_Armenia "Tiridates III of Armenia"), following his conversion by [Gregory the Illuminator](/wiki/Gregory_the_Illuminator "Gregory the Illuminator"). This makes Armenia the first nation to adopt Christianity as a state religion, although the exact date has been subject to scholarly debate.[[35]](#cite_note-35) In 380, three Roman emperors issued the [Edict of Thessalonica](/wiki/Edict_of_Thessalonica "Edict of Thessalonica") (*Cunctos populos*), making the Roman Empire a Christian state,[[5]](#cite_note-Ashby2010-5) and establishing [Nicene Christianity](/wiki/Nicene_Christianity "Nicene Christianity"), in the form of its [State Church](/wiki/State_church_of_the_Roman_Empire "State church of the Roman Empire"), as its official religion.[[36]](#cite_note-IsmaelIsmael2015-36)

After the fall of the Western Roman Empire in the late 5th century, the [Eastern Roman Empire](/wiki/Eastern_Roman_Empire "Eastern Roman Empire") under the emperor [Justinian](/wiki/Justinian "Justinian") (reigned 527–565), became the world's predominant Christian state, based on Roman law, Greek culture, and the Greek language."[[6]](#cite_note-Frucht2004-6)[[37]](#cite_note-Spielvogel2013-37)[[38]](#cite_note-Truxillo2008-38) In this Christian state, in which nearly all of its subjects upheld faith in Jesus, an "enormous amount of artistic talent was poured into the construction of churches, church ceremonies, and church decoration".[[37]](#cite_note-Spielvogel2013-37) John Binns describes this era, writing that:[[39]](#cite_note-Binns2002-39)

> A new stage in the history of the Church began when not just localised communities but nations became Christian. The stage is associated with the conversion of Constantine and the beginnings of a Christian Empire, but the Byzantine Emperor was not the first ruler to lead his people into Christianity, thus setting up the first Christian state. That honour traditionally goes to the church of Armenia.[[39]](#cite_note-Binns2002-39)

— John Binns, An Introduction to the Christian Orthodox Churches

As a Christian state, Armenia "embraced Christianity as the religion of the King, the nobles, and the people".[[3]](#cite_note-MilmanMurdock1887-3) In 326, according to official tradition of the Georgian Orthodox Church, following the [conversion](/wiki/Conversion_to_Christianity "Conversion to Christianity") of [Mirian](/wiki/Mirian_III_of_Iberia "Mirian III of Iberia") and [Nana](/wiki/Nana_of_Iberia "Nana of Iberia"), the country of [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)") became a Christian state, the Emperor Constantine the Great sending clerics for baptising people. In the 4th century, in the [Kingdom of Aksum](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Aksum "Kingdom of Aksum"), after [Ezana](/wiki/Ezana_of_Axum "Ezana of Axum")'s conversion to the faith, this empire also became a Christian state.[[4]](#cite_note-ChingJarzombek2010-4)[[40]](#cite_note-StantonRamsamy2012-40)

In the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), efforts were made in order to establish a [Pan-Christianity](/wiki/Pan-Christianity "Pan-Christianity") state by uniting the countries within [Christendom](/wiki/Christendom "Christendom").[[41]](#cite_note-Snyder1990-41)[[42]](#cite_note-Snyder1984-42) [Christian nationalism](/wiki/Christian_nationalism "Christian nationalism") played a role in this era in which Christians felt the impulse to also recover those territories in which Christianity historically flourished, such as the [Holy Land](/wiki/Holy_Land "Holy Land") and North Africa.[[43]](#cite_note-USE2005-43)

The [First Great Awakening](/wiki/First_Great_Awakening "First Great Awakening"), [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"), and [Second Great Awakening](/wiki/Second_Great_Awakening "Second Great Awakening") caused two rounds of disestablishment among the states of the new United States, from 1776 to 1833.[[44]](#cite_note-mtsu-44)

## Modern era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Modern era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/State_Religions.svg/500px-State_Religions.svg.png)](/wiki/File:State_Religions.svg)

Countries with Christianity as their state religion are in blue.

### Argentina

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Argentina")]

Article 2 of the [Constitution of Argentina](/wiki/Constitution_of_Argentina "Constitution of Argentina") explicitly states that "the Federal Government supports the Roman Catholic Apostolic Faith" and Article 14 guarantees [freedom of religion](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion "Freedom of religion").[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentinaarts._14,_20-47) Although it enforces neither an official nor a state faith,[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFayt1985347Bidart_Campos200553-48) it gives [Catholic Christianity](/wiki/Catholic_Christianity "Catholic Christianity") a preferential status.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_Argentinaart._2-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-irfr1-51) Before its [1994 amendment](/wiki/1994_amendment_of_the_Constitution_of_Argentina "1994 amendment of the Constitution of Argentina"), the Constitution stated that the [President of the Republic](/wiki/President_of_Argentina "President of Argentina") must be a Roman Catholic.

### Armenia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Armenia")]

In [Armenia](/wiki/Armenia "Armenia"), [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") is the most adhered religion, it has massive significance, but it is not the state religion. The [Armenian Apostolic Church](/wiki/Armenian_Apostolic_Church "Armenian Apostolic Church") is the [national church](/wiki/National_church "National church"), it has long kept the Armenian identity alive under various Empires. [Armenia](/wiki/Armenia "Armenia") is the first country which recognised Christianity as a state religion.

### Costa Rica

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Costa Rica")]

The constitution of [Costa Rica](/wiki/Costa_Rica "Costa Rica") states that "The Catholic and Apostolic Religion is the religion of the State".[[7]](#cite_note-YakobsonRubinstein2009-7) As such, Catholic Christian [holy days](/wiki/Holy_day_of_obligation "Holy day of obligation") are recognized by the government and "public schools provide [religious education](/wiki/Religious_education "Religious education")", although parents are able to opt-out their children if they choose to do so.[[52]](#cite_note-Merriman2009-52)

### Denmark

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Denmark")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Holte_Kirke_2005.jpg/250px-Holte_Kirke_2005.jpg)](/wiki/File:Holte_Kirke_2005.jpg)

A [Church of Denmark](/wiki/Church_of_Denmark "Church of Denmark") parish church in [Holte](/wiki/Holte "Holte"), with the [Dannebrog](/wiki/Flag_of_Denmark "Flag of Denmark") flying in its [kirkyard](/wiki/Kirkyard "Kirkyard")

As early as the 11th century AD, "Denmark was considered to be a Christian state",[[53]](#cite_note-WarburgChristoffersen2013-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54) with the [Church of Denmark](/wiki/Church_of_Denmark "Church of Denmark"), a member of the [Lutheran World Federation](/wiki/Lutheran_World_Federation "Lutheran World Federation"), being the state church.[[55]](#cite_note-55) Prof. Wasif Shadid, of [Leiden University](/wiki/Leiden_University "Leiden University"), writes that:

> The Lutheran established church is a department of the state. Church affairs are governed by a central government ministry, while clergy are government employees. The registration of births, deaths and marriages falls under this ministry of church affairs, and normally speaking the local Lutheran pastor is also the official registrar.[[8]](#cite_note-Shadid1995-8)

— W. A. R. Shadid, Religious Freedom and the Position of Islam in Western Europe, page 11

Over 82% of the population of Denmark are members of the [Evangelical Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") Church of Denmark, which is "officially headed by the queen of Denmark".[[56]](#cite_note-JuergensmeyerRoof2011-56) The [Act of Succession](/wiki/Act_of_Succession_(Denmark) "Act of Succession (Denmark)") specifies that monarch "shall be a member of the Evangelical Church."[[24]](#cite_note-Cimino2025-24) Furthermore, clergy "in the Church of Denmark are civil servants employed by the Ministry of Ecclesiastical Affairs" and the "economic base of the Church of Denmark is state-collected church taxes combined with a direct state subsidiary (12%), which symbolically covers the expenses of the Church of Denmark to run the civil registration and the burial system for all citizens."[[56]](#cite_note-JuergensmeyerRoof2011-56)

### England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: England")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Canterbury_Cathedral_-_Portal_Nave_Cross-spire.jpeg/250px-Canterbury_Cathedral_-_Portal_Nave_Cross-spire.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Canterbury_Cathedral_-_Portal_Nave_Cross-spire.jpeg)

[Canterbury Cathedral](/wiki/Canterbury_Cathedral "Canterbury Cathedral") houses the *[cathedra](/wiki/Cathedra "Cathedra")* of the [Archbishop of Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury"), the *[primus inter pares](/wiki/Primus_inter_pares "Primus inter pares")* of the worldwide [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion")

[Barbara Yorke](/wiki/Barbara_Yorke "Barbara Yorke") writes that the "[Carolingian Renaissance](/wiki/Carolingian_Renaissance "Carolingian Renaissance") heightened appreciation within England of the role of king and church in a Christian state."[[57]](#cite_note-Yorke2002-57) As such,

> Since the [1701 Act of Establishment](/wiki/Act_of_Settlement_1701 "Act of Settlement 1701"), England's official state church has been the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), the monarch being its [supreme governor](/wiki/Supreme_Governor_of_the_Church_of_England "Supreme Governor of the Church of England") and '[defender of the faith](/wiki/Defender_of_the_faith "Defender of the faith")'. [He](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III"), together with Parliament, has a say in appointing [bishops](/wiki/Anglican_ministry "Anglican ministry"), twenty-six of whom have ex officio seats in the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"). In characteristically British fashion, where the state is representative of civil society, it was Parliament that determined, in the Act of Establishment, that the monarch had to be [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican").[[9]](#cite_note-Joppke2013-9)

— Christian Joppke, page 1

Christian [religious education](/wiki/Religious_education_in_primary_and_secondary_education "Religious education in primary and secondary education") is taught to children in primary and secondary schools in the United Kingdom.[[58]](#cite_note-Eberle-58) [English schools](/wiki/Education_in_England "Education in England") have a legal requirement for a daily act of collective worship "of a broadly Christian character"[[59]](#cite_note-López-MuñizGroof2006-59) that is widely flouted.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

### Dominican Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Dominican Republic")]

The [Dominican Republic](/wiki/Dominican_Republic "Dominican Republic") is a Christian state, with Catholic Christianity being the official religion.[[10]](#cite_note-DOS2025-10) In view of the same, the government of the Dominican Republic extends special privileges to the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church").[[10]](#cite_note-DOS2025-10) National holidays include holy days of Christianity, such as the [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)") (January 6), [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday"), [Corpus Christi](/wiki/Feast_of_Corpus_Christi "Feast of Corpus Christi"), and [Christmas Day](/wiki/Christmas_Day "Christmas Day"). In the Dominican Republic, [religious education](/wiki/Religious_education "Religious education") classes must be of either a Catholic or evangelical Protestant basis and are required be taught in all elementary and secondary public schools.[[10]](#cite_note-DOS2025-10)

### Faroe Islands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Faroe Islands")]

The [Church of the Faroe Islands](/wiki/Church_of_the_Faroe_Islands "Church of the Faroe Islands") is the state church of [Faroe Islands](/wiki/Faroe_Islands "Faroe Islands").[[61]](#cite_note-StatisticsFaroe-61)

### Georgia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Georgia")]

[Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)") is one of the oldest Christian states. Article 8 of Georgian Constitution and [the Concordat of 2002](/wiki/Concordat_of_2002 "Concordat of 2002") grants [the Georgian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Georgian_Orthodox_Church "Georgian Orthodox Church") special privileges, which include legal immunity to [the Patriarch of Georgia](/wiki/Patriarch_of_Georgia "Patriarch of Georgia"). The Orthodox Church is the most trusted institution in the country[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63) and its head, Patriarch [Ilia II](/wiki/Ilia_II_of_Georgia "Ilia II of Georgia"), the most trusted person.[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65)

### Greece

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Greece")]

[Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") is a Christian state,[[12]](#cite_note-Jiang2012-12)[[66]](#cite_note-EnyediMadeley2004-66) with the [Church of Greece](/wiki/Church_of_Greece "Church of Greece") playing "a dominant role in the life of the country".[[67]](#cite_note-Meyendorff1981-67)

[Mount Athos](/wiki/Mount_Athos "Mount Athos") and most of the Athos peninsula are governed as an [autonomous region](/wiki/Autonomous_administrative_division "Autonomous administrative division") in Greece by the [monastic community of Mount Athos](/wiki/Monastic_community_of_Mount_Athos "Monastic community of Mount Athos"), which is ecclesiastically under the direct jurisdiction of the [Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople](/wiki/Ecumenical_Patriarchate_of_Constantinople "Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople").

### Greenland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Greenland")]

Being an autonomous [constituent country](/wiki/Denmark#Greenland_and_the_Faroe_Islands "Denmark") within the [Kingdom of Denmark](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Denmark "Kingdom of Denmark"), the [Church of Denmark](/wiki/Church_of_Denmark "Church of Denmark") is the [established church](/wiki/State_religion "State religion") of [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland") through the [Constitution of Denmark](/wiki/Constitution_of_Denmark "Constitution of Denmark"):

> The Evangelical Lutheran Church shall be the Established Church of Denmark, and, as such, it shall be supported by the State.

— Section IV of [Constitution of Denmark](/wiki/Constitution_of_Denmark "Constitution of Denmark")[[68]](#cite_note-DenmarkConstitution-68)

This applies toof the Kingdom of Denmark, except for the [Faroe Islands](/wiki/Faroe_Islands "Faroe Islands"), as the [Church of the Faroe Islands](/wiki/Church_of_the_Faroe_Islands "Church of the Faroe Islands") became independent in 2007.

### Hungary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Hungary")]

The preamble to the [Hungarian Constitution of 2011](/wiki/Constitution_of_Hungary "Constitution of Hungary") describes Hungary as "part of Christian Europe" and acknowledges "the role of Christianity in preserving nationhood", while Article VII provides that "the State shall cooperate with the Churches for community goals". However, the constitution also guarantees freedom of religion and separation of church and state.[[13]](#cite_note-Hungary's_Constitution_of_2011-13)

### Iceland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Iceland")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/D%C3%B3mkirkjan_%C3%AD_Reykjav%C3%ADk_2023.jpg/250px-D%C3%B3mkirkjan_%C3%AD_Reykjav%C3%ADk_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:D%C3%B3mkirkjan_%C3%AD_Reykjav%C3%ADk_2023.jpg)

[Reykjavík Cathedral](/wiki/Reykjav%C3%ADk_Cathedral "Reykjavík Cathedral"), [mother Church](/wiki/Mother_Church "Mother Church") of the Evangelical Lutheran Church of Iceland

Around AD 1000, [Iceland became a Christian state](/wiki/Christianization_of_Iceland "Christianization of Iceland").[[69]](#cite_note-Kendrick2012-69) The *Encyclopedia of Protestantism* states that:

> The majority of Icelanders are members of the state church. Almost all children are [baptized](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism") as [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") and more than 90 percent are subsequently [confirmed](/wiki/Confirmation_(Lutheran_Church) "Confirmation (Lutheran Church)"). The church conducts 75 percent of all marriages and 99 percent of all funerals. The [Evangelical Lutheran Church in Iceland](/wiki/Church_of_Iceland "Church of Iceland") is a member of the [Lutheran World Federation](/wiki/Lutheran_World_Federation "Lutheran World Federation") and the [World Council of Churches](/wiki/World_Council_of_Churches "World Council of Churches").[[14]](#cite_note-Melton2005-14)

— J. Gordon Melton, Encyclopedia of Protestantism, page 283

All public schools have mandatory education in [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), although an exemption may be considered by the Minister of Education.[[70]](#cite_note-Fox-70)

### Liechtenstein

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Liechtenstein")]

[Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein "Liechtenstein")'s constitution designates the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") as being the state Church of that country.[[15]](#cite_note-Fox2008-15) In public schools, per article 16 of the [Constitution of Liechtenstein](/wiki/Constitution_of_Liechtenstein "Constitution of Liechtenstein"), religious education is given by Church authorities.[[15]](#cite_note-Fox2008-15)

### Malta

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Malta")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Civil_Ensign_of_Malta.svg/250px-Civil_Ensign_of_Malta.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Civil_Ensign_of_Malta.svg)

The [Civil Ensign of Malta](/wiki/Flag_of_Malta "Flag of Malta")

Section Two of the [Constitution of Malta](/wiki/Constitution_of_Malta "Constitution of Malta") specifies the state's religion as being the Roman Catholic Apostolic Religion.[[71]](#cite_note-Gozdecka2015-71)[[16]](#cite_note-legal-malta-16) It holds that the "authorities of the Roman Catholic Apostolic Church have the duty to teach which principles are right and which are wrong" and that "religious teaching of the Roman Catholic Apostolic Faith shall be provided in all State schools as part of compulsory education".[[71]](#cite_note-Gozdecka2015-71)

### Monaco

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Monaco")]

Article 9 of the [Constitution of Monaco](/wiki/Constitution_of_Monaco "Constitution of Monaco") describes "*La religion catholique, apostolique et romaine* [the catholic, [apostolic](/wiki/Apostolic_succession "Apostolic succession") and Roman religion]" as the religion of the state.[[17]](#cite_note-MonacoReligion-17)

### Norway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Norway")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Norwegian_Bible.jpg/250px-Norwegian_Bible.jpg)](/wiki/File:Norwegian_Bible.jpg)

The [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible") in the [Norwegian language](/wiki/Norwegian_language "Norwegian language")

[Cole Durham](/wiki/Cole_Durham "Cole Durham") and Tore Sam Lindholm, writing in 2013, stated that "For a period of one thousand years Norway has been a kingdom with a Christian *state church*" and that a decree went out in 1739 ordering that "Elementary schooling for all Norwegian children became mandatory, so that all Norwegians should be able to read the Bible and the [Lutheran Catechism](/wiki/Luther%27s_Large_Catechism "Luther's Large Catechism") firsthand."[[72]](#cite_note-DurhamLindholm2013-72) The modern [Constitution of Norway](/wiki/Constitution_of_Norway "Constitution of Norway") stipulates that "[The Church of Norway](/wiki/Church_of_Norway "Church of Norway"), an Evangelical-Lutheran church, will remain the Established Church of Norway and will as such be supported by the State."[[73]](#cite_note-73) As such, the "Norwegian constitution decrees that [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") is the official religion of the State and that the King is the supreme temporal head of the Church."[[74]](#cite_note-EriksenAfrikainstitutet2000-74)[[75]](#cite_note-Singh2008-75) The administration of the Church "is shared between the Ministry for Church, Education and Research centrally and municipal authorities locally",[[74]](#cite_note-EriksenAfrikainstitutet2000-74) and the Church of Norway "depends on state and local taxes".[[76]](#cite_note-Fahlbusch2003-76) The Church of Norway is responsible for the "maintenance of church buildings and cemeteries".[[77]](#cite_note-77) In the mid-20th century, the vast majority of Norwegians participated in the Lutheran Church. According to a 1957 description, "[o]ver 90 percent of the population are married by state church clergymen, have their children [baptized](/wiki/Lutheran_sacraments#Baptism "Lutheran sacraments") and [confirmed](/wiki/Confirmation_(Lutheran_Church) "Confirmation (Lutheran Church)"), and finally are [buried](/wiki/Christian_burial "Christian burial") with a church service."[[78]](#cite_note-Flint1957-78) However, current membership in the Evangelical Lutheran [Church of Norway](/wiki/Church_of_Norway "Church of Norway") is lower, standing at 65% of the population in 2021.[[79]](#cite_note-stat2021statechurch-79)

In 2017, the Church of Norway was made self-governing, with the identity of the denomination shifting from a state church to a [national church](/wiki/National_church "National church").[[80]](#cite_note-AskelandSchmidt2016-80) The Church of Noway continues to be supported by public funding.[[80]](#cite_note-AskelandSchmidt2016-80) Succession rules governing the [Monarchy of Norway](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Norway "Monarchy of Norway") require that the monarch be an [Evangelical Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") holding membership in the Church of Norway.[[25]](#cite_note-Fandel2007-25) Those who marry into the royal family of Norway are expected to be or become Evangelical Lutherans.[[25]](#cite_note-Fandel2007-25)

### Samoa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Samoa")]

[Samoa](/wiki/Samoa "Samoa") became a Christian state in 2017. Article 1 of the Samoan Constitution states that “Samoa is a Christian nation founded of God the Father, the Son and the Holy Spirit”.[[18]](#cite_note-Wyeth2017-18)

### Serbia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Serbia")]

Main articles: [Religion in Serbia](/wiki/Religion_in_Serbia "Religion in Serbia"), [Eastern Orthodoxy in Serbia](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy_in_Serbia "Eastern Orthodoxy in Serbia"), and [Serbian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Serbian_Orthodox_Church "Serbian Orthodox Church")

[Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia") as a territory became a Christian state during the time of [Constantine the Great](/wiki/Constantine_the_Great "Constantine the Great") in [Christianization](/wiki/Christianization_of_Rome "Christianization of Rome") of [Eastern Roman Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"), according to the research and discoveries of artifacts left by the [Illyrians](/wiki/Illyrians "Illyrians"), [Triballi](/wiki/Triballi "Triballi") and other kindred tribes. More research has since been made that perhaps prove the existence of [Serbs](/wiki/Serbs "Serbs") living in the Balkans during Roman times in [Ilyria](/wiki/Ilyria "Ilyria"). In the centuries that followed from the 4th to the 12th century, when [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") was in a battleground between Serbia due the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Theological_differences_between_the_Catholic_Church_and_the_Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Theological differences between the Catholic Church and the Eastern Orthodox Church"), Serbia prevailed as [Orthodox Christian](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") state under his [jurisdiction](/wiki/Serbian_Orthodox_Church "Serbian Orthodox Church") through [Saint Sava](/wiki/Saint_Sava "Saint Sava").[[81]](#cite_note-81)

Serbia as modern state, defines in its [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Serbia "Constitution of Serbia") as a [secular state](/wiki/Secular_state "Secular state") with guaranteed [religious freedom](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion "Freedom of religion").[[82]](#cite_note-82) However, Orthodox Christians with 6,079,396 adherents comprise 84.5% of the country's population. The [Serbian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Serbian_Orthodox_Church "Serbian Orthodox Church") is the largest traditional church of the country, adherents of which are overwhelmingly [Serbs](/wiki/Serbs "Serbs"). The SOC directly or indirectly has [cultural](/wiki/Culture "Culture") influence on both the [decisions and positions of the state](/wiki/Politics_of_Serbia "Politics of Serbia").[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84)[[85]](#cite_note-85)

### Sweden

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Sweden")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Konsekration_Alsike_Kyrka.png/330px-Konsekration_Alsike_Kyrka.png)](/wiki/File:Konsekration_Alsike_Kyrka.png)

Lutheran priest [elevating](/wiki/Elevation_(liturgy) "Elevation (liturgy)") the [host](/wiki/Sacramental_bread "Sacramental bread") during the offering of the [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") at [Alsike Church](/wiki/Alsike_Church "Alsike Church"), Sweden

Under the reign of [Gustav Vasa](/wiki/Gustav_Vasa "Gustav Vasa"), Sweden became an [Evangelical-Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") Christian country.[[86]](#cite_note-GassmannOldenburg2011-86) The Protestant Reformation in Sweden was led by the Evangelical-Lutheran divines [Olaus Petri](/wiki/Olaus_Petri "Olaus Petri") and [Laurentius Petri](/wiki/Laurentius_Petri "Laurentius Petri").[[86]](#cite_note-GassmannOldenburg2011-86) Laurentius Petri was consecrated in the [apostolic succession](/wiki/Apostolic_succession "Apostolic succession") that, according to the [Swedish Church Ordinance 1571](/wiki/Swedish_Church_Ordinance_1571 "Swedish Church Ordinance 1571") "must remain in the future, so long as the world lasts."[[86]](#cite_note-GassmannOldenburg2011-86) In 1544, the Parliament in Västerås declared Sweden to be an Evangelical-Lutheran kingdom.[[86]](#cite_note-GassmannOldenburg2011-86) The [Kyrkogångsplikt](/wiki/Kyrkog%C3%A5ngsplikt "Kyrkogångsplikt") was the legal obligation for Swedish citizens to attend [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy)#Lutheranism "Mass (liturgy)") every [Lord's Day](/wiki/Lord%27s_Day "Lord's Day") in the Evangelical Lutheran church. The Evangelical-Lutheran [Church of Sweden](/wiki/Church_of_Sweden "Church of Sweden") historically served as the state church, though in 2000, this relationship was loosened; "the Church of Sweden is still legally regulated by the state, and the monarch must be a member and assent to its theology."[[87]](#cite_note-DemyShaw2019-87) The [Swedish Act of Succession](/wiki/Swedish_Act_of_Succession "Swedish Act of Succession") requires monarchs to "profess the pure evangelical faith".[[26]](#cite_note-KrunkeThorarensen2018-26)

### Tonga

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Tonga")]

[Tonga](/wiki/Tonga "Tonga") became a Christian state under [George Tupou I](/wiki/George_Tupou_I "George Tupou I") in the 19th century,[[20]](#cite_note-Fodor1986-20)[[88]](#cite_note-Oliver1989-88) with the [Free Wesleyan Church](/wiki/Free_Wesleyan_Church "Free Wesleyan Church"), a member of the [World Methodist Council](/wiki/World_Methodist_Council "World Methodist Council"), being established as the country's state Church.[[89]](#cite_note-Bell2005-89) Under the rule of George Tupou I, there was established a "rigorous constitutional clause regulating observation of the [Sabbath](/wiki/Sabbath_in_Christianity "Sabbath in Christianity")".[[20]](#cite_note-Fodor1986-20)

### Tuvalu

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Tuvalu")]

The [Church of Tuvalu](/wiki/Church_of_Tuvalu "Church of Tuvalu"), a [Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinist "Calvinist") church in the [Congregationalist](/wiki/Congregationalist "Congregationalist") tradition, is the state church of [Tuvalu](/wiki/Tuvalu "Tuvalu") and was established as such in 1991.[[90]](#cite_note-Ferrari2015-90) The [Constitution of Tuvalu](/wiki/Constitution_of_Tuvalu "Constitution of Tuvalu") identifies Tuvalu as "an independent State based on Christian principles".[[21]](#cite_note-Temperman2010-21)

### Vatican City

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Vatican City")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/StPetersBasilicaEarlyMorning.jpg/500px-StPetersBasilicaEarlyMorning.jpg)](/wiki/File:StPetersBasilicaEarlyMorning.jpg)

[St. Peter's Square](/wiki/St._Peter%27s_Square "St. Peter's Square"), Vatican City

[Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City") is a Christian state, in which the "[Pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope") is *ex officio* simultaneously leader of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") as well as Head of State and Head of the Government of the State of the Vatican City; he also possesses (*de jure*) absolute authority over the legislative, executive and judicial branches."[[22]](#cite_note-Erasmus2010-22)

### Zambia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Zambia")]

Jeroen Temperman, a professor of international law at [Erasmus University Rotterdam](/wiki/Erasmus_University_Rotterdam "Erasmus University Rotterdam") writes that:

> [Zambia](/wiki/Zambia "Zambia") is officially a Christian state as well, though the legal ramifications clearly do not compare to the latter state. The Preamble of the [Constitution of Zambia](/wiki/Constitution_of_Zambia "Constitution of Zambia") establishes Zambia as a Christian state without specifying "Christian" denominationally. It simply proclaims: "We, the people of Zambia...declare the Republic a Christian nation..." As far as state practice is concerned, it may be pointed out that the Government maintains relations with the Zambian Council of Churches and requires Christianity to be taught in the public school curriculum.[[91]](#cite_note-Rotterdam2010-91)

— Jeroen Temperman, State-Religion Relationships and Human Rights Law, page 18

After "Zambia declared itself a Christian nation in 1991", "the nation's vice president urged citizens to 'have a [Christian orientation](/wiki/Christian_worldview "Christian worldview") in all fields, at all levels'."[[23]](#cite_note-Jenkins2011-23)

## National church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_state&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: National church")]

Main article: [National church](/wiki/National_church "National church")

A number of countries have a [national church](/wiki/National_church "National church") which is not established (as the official religion of the nation), but is nonetheless recognised under civil law as being the country's acknowledged religious denomination. Whilst these are not Christian states, the official Christian national church is likely to have certain residual state functions in relation to state occasions and ceremonial. Examples include Scotland ([Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland")) and Sweden ([Church of Sweden](/wiki/Church_of_Sweden "Church of Sweden")). A national church typically has a monopoly on official state recognition, although unusually [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland") has two national churches (the [Evangelical Lutheran Church of Finland](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Church_of_Finland "Evangelical Lutheran Church of Finland") and the [Finnish Orthodox Church](/wiki/Finnish_Orthodox_Church "Finnish Orthodox Church")), both recognised under civil law as joint official churches of the nation.

1. **[^](#cite_ref-95)** [Brazilian Laws - the Federal Constitution - The Organization of State](http://www.v-brazil.com/government/laws/titleIII.html). V-brazil.com. Retrieved 5 May 2012. [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") had [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism "Roman Catholicism") as the state religion from the country's [independence](/wiki/Independence_of_Brazil "Independence of Brazil"), in 1822, until the fall of the [Brazilian Empire](/wiki/Brazilian_Empire "Brazilian Empire"). The [new Republican government](/wiki/Rep%C3%BAblica_Velha "República Velha") passed, in 1890, Decree 119-A ["Decreto 119-A"](http://www.planalto.gov.br/ccivil_03/decreto/1851-1899/d119-a.htm). "Prohibits federal and state authorities to intervene on religion, granting freedom of religion." (still in force), instituting the separation of church and state for the first time in Brazilian law. [Positivist](/wiki/Positivist "Positivist") thinker [Demétrio Nunes Ribeiro](/w/index.php?title=Dem%C3%A9trio_Nunes_Ribeiro&action=edit&redlink=1 "Demétrio Nunes Ribeiro (page does not exist)") urged the new government to adopt this stance. The 1891 Constitution, the first under the Republican system of government, abolished privileges for any specific religion, reaffirming the separation of church and state. This has been the case ever since – the 1988 [Constitution of Brazil](/wiki/Constitution_of_Brazil "Constitution of Brazil"), currently in force, does so in its Nineteenth Article. The Preamble to the Constitution does refer to "God's protection" over the document's [promulgation](/wiki/Promulgation "Promulgation"), but this is not legally taken as endorsement of belief in any deity.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-100)** In France the [Concordat of 1801](/wiki/Concordat_of_1801 "Concordat of 1801") made the Catholic, [Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinist "Calvinist") and [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") churches state-sponsored religions, as well as [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism").
3. **[^](#cite_ref-103)** In Hungary the constitutional laws of 1848 declared five established churches on equal status: the [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic"), [Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinist "Calvinist"), [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran"), [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox") and [Unitarian](/wiki/Unitarianism "Unitarianism") Church. In 1868 the law was ratified again after the [Ausgleich](/wiki/Ausgleich "Ausgleich"). In 1895 [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") was also recognized as the sixth established church. In 1948 every distinction between the different denominations were abolished.[[99]](#cite_note-101)[[100]](#cite_note-102)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-105)** In the [Kingdom of Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") the [Church of Ireland](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland") was established [in the Reformation](/wiki/Reformation_in_Ireland "Reformation in Ireland").[[101]](#cite_note-LivingstoneSparks2013-104) The [Act of Union 1800](/wiki/Act_of_Union_1800 "Act of Union 1800") created the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") with the [United Church of England and Ireland](/wiki/United_Church_of_England_and_Ireland "United Church of England and Ireland") established outside Scotland. The [Irish Church Act 1869](/wiki/Irish_Church_Act_1869 "Irish Church Act 1869") demerged and disestablished the Church of Ireland,[[101]](#cite_note-LivingstoneSparks2013-104) and the island was [partitioned in 1922](/wiki/Partition_of_Ireland "Partition of Ireland").
5. **[^](#cite_ref-109)** The Republic of Ireland's [1937 constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Ireland "Constitution of Ireland") prohibits an established religion.[[102]](#cite_note-106) Originally, it recognized the "special position" of the Catholic Church "as the guardian of the Faith professed by [the great majority of the citizens](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism_in_Ireland "Roman Catholicism in Ireland")", and recognized "the [Church of Ireland](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland"), the [Presbyterian Church in Ireland](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_Ireland "Presbyterian Church in Ireland"), the [Methodist Church in Ireland](/wiki/Methodist_Church_in_Ireland "Methodist Church in Ireland"), the [Religious Society of Friends in Ireland](/wiki/Religious_Society_of_Friends_in_Ireland "Religious Society of Friends in Ireland"), as well as the [Jewish Congregations](/wiki/Jews_in_Ireland "Jews in Ireland") and the other religious denominations existing in Ireland at the date of the coming into operation of this Constitution".[[103]](#cite_note-KeoghMcCarthy2007-107) These provisions were [deleted in 1973](/wiki/Fifth_Amendment_of_the_Constitution_of_Ireland "Fifth Amendment of the Constitution of Ireland").[[104]](#cite_note-108)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-113)** The Philippines was among several possessions ceded by [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") to the United States in 1898; religious freedom was subsequently guaranteed in the archipelago. This was codified in the [Philippine Organic Act (1902)](/wiki/Philippine_Organic_Act_(1902) "Philippine Organic Act (1902)"), section 5: "... That no law shall be made respecting an establishment of religion or prohibiting the free exercise thereof, and that the free exercise and enjoyment of religious profession and worship, without discrimination or preference, shall forever be allowed." A similarly worded provision still exists in the [present Constitution](/wiki/1987_Constitution "1987 Constitution"). Catholicism remains the predominant religion, wielding considerable political and cultural influence.
7. **[^](#cite_ref-114)** Article 25 of [the constitution](http://www.sejm.gov.pl/prawo/konst/angielski/kon1.htm) states: "1. Churches and other religious organizations shall have equal rights. 2. Public authorities in the Republic of Poland shall be impartial in matters of personal conviction". Article 114 of the Polish [March Constitution of 1921](/wiki/March_Constitution_of_Poland "March Constitution of Poland") declared the Catholic Church to hold "the principal position among religious denominations equal before the law" (in reference to the idea of *[first among equals](/wiki/Primus_inter_pares "Primus inter pares")*). The article was continued in force by article 81 of the [April Constitution of 1935](/wiki/April_Constitution_of_Poland "April Constitution of Poland"). The Soviet-backed [PKWN Manifesto](/wiki/PKWN_Manifesto "PKWN Manifesto") of 1944 reintroduced the March Constitution, which remained in force until it was replaced by the [Small Constitution of 1947](/wiki/Small_Constitution_of_1947 "Small Constitution of 1947").
8. **[^](#cite_ref-117)** The [Church in Wales](/wiki/Church_in_Wales "Church in Wales") was split from the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") in 1920, by [Welsh Church Act 1914](/wiki/Welsh_Church_Act_1914 "Welsh Church Act 1914"); at the same time becoming disestablished.
